titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Dorothy Gray Papers
titlestmt.author: Kolbe Resnick

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 27, 2024 at 23:58

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract:
The Dorothy Gray papers consist of correspondence, photographic prints and negatives, photograph albums, musical scores, clippings, appointment books, and files created by and related to Dorothy Gray and her life in Hollywood as a child actress and talent manager.
Included in the collection are family photographs going back to the late 19th century, as well as photographic prints and negatives of Gray and friends, pets, and trips she took around the world. A bulk of the collection documents Gray's romantic relationships with two women, Ann Hathaway and Kellie Greene, through letters and photographs.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from William Reese Co. on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged into five series: Series I: Correspondence, 1951–1968 Series II: Scrapbooks and notebooks, circa 1960-1994 Series III: Photographs, 1890–1960 Series IV: Business materials, 1950–1969 Series V: Other materials, 1975.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Dorothy Gray (born October 23, 1922), actress, talent manager, and assistant to Katherine Dunham, grew up in Los Angeles, CA. The niece of Charlie Chaplin, Gray became a child actress at a very young age, appearing in films as early as 1924. She was later cast in a number of Hollywood productions, including uncredited roles in Symphony of Six Million (1932), Men in White (1934), and Rose-Marie (1936). In the 1940s she started working with jazz musicians and stage performers in Hollywood as an assistant and manager. When Gray was nineteen, she met Katherine Dunham at the Paramount Theater in Los Angeles. The two formed a close bond that was "romantic, sisterly, and maternal" (Das 2017). Dorothy Gray managed Katherine Dunham's business and bookings in the United States until her death in 1976. From the 1950s onward Gray was in two long term romantic relationships with two women, Ann Hathaway and Kellie Greene. Dorothy Gray died May 9, 1976, in Los Angeles, California.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Dorothy Gray Papers. James Weldon Johnson Collection in the Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Archival Description Unit Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Dorothy Gray papers span the years 1890-1994 and consist of correspondence, photographic prints and negatives, photograph albums, musical scores, clippings, appointment books, and files related to her life in Hollywood and her career as a talent manager, including scrapbooks following the careers of friends and associates. The collection also documents her family and childhood.

A bulk of the collection material relates to her relationships with her social circle in California during the years 1951-1968. Included are love letters Gray exchanged with pianist Kellie Greene and jazz singer Ann Hathaway as well as photographs of their travels, family, and pets.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Dorothy Gray papers are the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related material: Katherine Dunham collection (JWJ MSS 247).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. Correspondence consists of letters and greeting cards received by Dorothy Gray. The letters touch on business matters related to professional life in Hollywood and with traveling performers, including one folder of letters from Katherine Dunham. The bulk of the letters document her romantic relationships with two women: Ann Hathaway and Kellie Greene.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. Scrapbooks contains three scrapbooks documenting Gray's personal and professional life, with one compiled posthumously. All three contain material related to and documenting clients, performances, and members of Gray's social circle in Hollywood and beyond. The material in the scrapbooks includes photographs, clippings, ephemera, playbills, notes, and xeroxed advertisements.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III. Photographs includes both photographic prints and negatives. Subjects of the photographs range from friends and family, travel photography, as well as many photographs of Gray's pets, specifically her dogs. There are professional, 8x10 headshots as well as smaller, black and white snapshots, largely documenting Gray's life in the 1940s and 1950s. Also included are photographs removed from a photograph album, including photographs of family and Gray's childhood.

did.unittitle: Ann Hathaway, Central Park, New York City
Thelma's color negatives of Inside U.S.A.
Nitrate negatives of a trip to Arizona

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV. Business materials consists of paper materials related to Gray's career as a business manager. Included in this series are appointment books, contact book, and notes, clippings, and ephemera documenting reviews or performances regarding or relevant to Gray's clients.
